name: Participant Admission Pathway Rigor
decision_axis: This lever controls event-yield probability and safety screening burden by choosing between rapid remote pre-screening, in-person multi-disciplinary adjudication, or a hybrid step-gate that escalates skeptical cases.
consequences: Stricter pre-admission adjudication (specialist interview, collateral history, prior PSG, PI sign-off) increases recruitment latency and costs, but raises the likelihood that admitted participants will produce captured events during their stay, reducing zero-yield unproductive admissions. Conversely, looser remote screening accelerates enrollment but risks admitting participants whose parasomnias are infrequent, misreported, or mimicked by epilepsy or other conditions, wasting suite-nights and degrading dataset quality. The 20% unproductive-admission ceiling hinges on this decision.
options: Require all candidates to undergo a structured clinical interview and collateral history verification in Bonn before consent, with PI adjudication of exclusion criteria on site; accept 4–6 week recruitment cycles but maximize event yield and safety.

Accept remote pre-screening (questionnaire, prior polysomnography report, telephone interview) to accelerate admission, then escalate only borderline cases (suspected mimics, seizure-like events, unclear histories) to in-person PI review before consent.

Deploy a two-tier pathway: all candidates receive standardized telephone intake and remote video conference with a sleep psychologist; only those flagged for diagnostic uncertainty or safety concern proceed to full in-person adjudication and PI sign-off.
review: The core tension is that rapid recruitment relieves pressure on the 3-year enrollment target (50–70 participants) but raises unproductive-admission risk, potentially triggering protocol review mid-cycle. None of the options explicitly cost out the trade-off between investigator time (PI adjudication hours) and participant time (in-person travel); if remote pre-screening is adopted, the facility risks false-negative exclusions (admitting epilepsy mimics undetected until night-one EEG), which compounds staffing and safety liability.

name: Tiered Sensor Deployment Cadence
decision_axis: This lever controls data richness and technician annotation burden by choosing between continuous full PSG from night one, continuous lightweight monitoring with PSG escalation only on detected events, or a scheduled PSG calendar (first, mid-stay, final nights) with continuous lightweight baseline.
consequences: Continuous full PSG maximizes data resolution but generates enormous annotation load; at 8 active suites with 8–10 hour monitoring nights, dual-rater scoring of continuous 19-channel PSG quickly becomes unmanageable without additional staff or algorithmic screening. The lightweight-continuous-with-escalation model (headband EEG, mattress sensor, infrared camera, microphone) reduces technician burden during quiet nights but depends on reliable real-time event flagging; if the technician misses or falsely flags an episode, the window for PSG capture closes. Scheduled PSG (first, mid-stay, final per protocol) balances clinical-grade capture against annotation workload and costs.
options: Acquire full 19-channel PSG (scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, leg EMG, cardiorespiratory, position sensors) every night for all 8 active suites; staff additional full-time scoring technician and allocate budget for independent rater time; accept €50–80K annual increase in equipment and labor.

Stream lightweight sensors (dry-electrode headband EEG, pressure-sensitive mattress, infrared video, ambient microphone) continuously; technician monitors real-time output for event indicators (sudden movement, arousal markers, audio anomalies) and triggers on-demand full PSG within 30 seconds of suspected event onset.

Deploy scheduled full PSG on protocol-fixed nights (admission night, one mid-stay night, discharge night) plus one unscheduled escalation night if no events captured during lightweight monitoring after 5 nights; technician annotates scheduled PSG recordings and lightweight nights receive secondary post-hoc review if events are suspected.
review: The core tension is balancing data fidelity (continuous PSG captures morphology nuance) against annotation capacity and budget realism; the facility has identified 3 research technicians for 8 suites, which already assumes compressed slack. Scheduled PSG with lightweight baseline is operationally sound, but the model assumes technician event detection during lightweight-only nights is reliable—if the technician consistently misses subtle arousals or confusional awakenings that lack explosive motor activity, the escalation pathway fails silently. None of the options address how the facility will validate technician sensitivity on lightweight monitoring before events become unrecoverable.

name: Video Data Governance and Repository Participation
decision_axis: This lever controls dataset reusability and institutional liability exposure by choosing between strict local-only video retention (deleted post-annotation), open de-identification and public deposit, or restricted-access data-use-agreement partnerships.
consequences: Local-only retention (video never leaves Bonn, deleted after manual annotation and de-identified sensor/EEG export) minimizes privacy litigation risk and aligns with German dormitory-expectation norms, but forecloses external algorithm validation and reanalysis by independent teams, limiting the facility's long-term scientific leverage. Public deposit with state-of-the-art de-identification (face blur, temporal shifting, re-keyed participant codes) enables broad reuse and citation but carries residual re-identification risk (gait, room layouts, ambient audio) and may deter some participants despite consent. Restricted partnerships (data-use agreements with a 2–3 institutional network sharing video under controlled governance) split the difference but introduce governance overhead and slow external collaboration.
options: Retain bedroom video only on local encrypted NAS; use video for manual annotation and algorithm development in-house; delete all video 24 months after final participant discharge; deposit only de-identified physiological (EEG, EMG) and metadata to a public repository under Creative Commons licenses.

Apply automated face de-identification, temporal anonymization (shuffle event timestamps by ±30 minutes), and room-identifier removal; deposit complete video and physiological dataset to Zenodo or OpenNeuro under restricted data-use agreements requiring institutional attestation and publication ethics review before access.

Establish a tiered-access consortium: local Bonn team retains unrestricted video for internal algorithm development; partner institutions (German and European sleep research centers) access video under institutional data-use agreements with publication-approval checkpoints; public repository receives only de-identified physiological/sensor streams and anonymized event metadata.
review: The core tension is whether video re-use risk justifies the operational and ethical overhead of repository participation, or whether local-only retention is ethically sufficient for methods-validation research. The facility's stated practice is to exclude video from public deposit, yet the plan does not explicitly weigh the cost to reproducibility: external sleep centers cannot validate the event-triage algorithm, cannot judge annotation decisions by visual inspection, and cannot pursue independent phenotyping questions. None of the options cost out the staff time required for face de-identification and temporal shifting, nor do they specify the approval criteria for data-use-agreement access, risking informal scope creep.

name: Semi-Automated Event-Triage Algorithm Development and Benchmarking
decision_axis: This lever controls the rate of return on annotation labor and algorithm maturity by choosing between rule-based sensor heuristics, supervised learning on hand-labeled events, or a hybrid incremental-learning pipeline that improves with each cohort.
consequences: Rule-based triage (e.g., threshold-crossing on headband EEG power, mattress pressure jumps, and audio amplitude) is fast to implement and interpretable but brittle to individual variation and sensor drift, producing high false-positive rates that do not reduce technician load meaningfully. Supervised learning on 200–400 hand-labeled events (early cohorts) trains a more general model but requires upfront annotation cost with no feedback to the technician until late in year one; model drift and class imbalance (parasomnias are rare vs. normal sleep) are common pitfalls. Incremental hybrid approaches (rule-based initialization, confidence-threshold escalation flagging uncertain cases for technician review, supervised retraining per cohort) amortize labeling cost and adapt over time.
options: Define rule-based heuristics (headband EEG alpha/theta ratios, mattress pressure velocity, video motion magnitude, microphone audio energy) with thresholds calibrated on the first 50 captured events; deploy as real-time technician alerts; update thresholds quarterly based on false-positive feedback but do not train supervised models.

Accumulate hand-labeled events from cohorts 1–3 (150–300 events); retrain a random-forest or gradient-boosted ensemble every 6 months with 10-fold cross-validation; evaluate sensitivity and specificity against dual human scorer agreement; freeze the model at month 24 and deploy as a static prediction tool in year three.

Initialize rule-based alerts for year-one rapid deployment; after cohort 1 (50+ events), train a lightweight supervised model (logistic regression or small neural network) on confidence-threshold uncertain cases identified by rules; retrain every cohort; accept 20% of alerts for human review and iteratively improve the separation boundary between confirmed events and false positives.
review: The core tension is between algorithmic sophistication and operational triage speed: rule-based systems deploy immediately but flood technicians with false alarms, while supervised learning delays deployment but risks class imbalance (far more normal sleep than parasomnia events in the dataset). The facility's success criterion (Aim 3) is a benchmarked algorithm with reported sensitivity and specificity against dual scoring, not autonomous diagnosis, but none of the options explicitly define the acceptable false-positive rate or specify how the model will be evaluated on held-out participants from different cohorts to assess generalization. If supervised retraining happens every 6 months but the algorithm is not frozen until month 24, the year-three benchmark may be inflated by training-set familiarity rather than true generalization.

name: Night Technician Shift Structure and Staffing Reserves
decision_axis: This lever controls response time to acute events, annotation accuracy, and staff burnout risk by choosing between continuous solo coverage per suite, paired technician teams with cross-suite monitoring, or on-call escalation with shared duty nights.
consequences: Continuous solo technician coverage of 8 suites (8 hours × 3 technicians rotating) ensures immediate response to safety events but creates high vigilance burden and unsustainable overnight shift schedules; technician fatigue increases annotation error and false-alarm rates. Paired teams (4 technicians per night, 2 pairs covering 4 suites each) reduce individual workload and enable peer annotation review but consume an additional €30–40K annually and leave the 8-suite expansion pathway understaffed. On-call escalation (one technician active, one on standby in a nearby hotel for event emergencies) reduces labor cost but delays response to acute motor events in participants with high injury risk, violating the safety-first operational mandate.
options: Deploy two technicians per shift (6-hour overlap coverage ensuring 8-suite monitoring redundancy); rotate three technicians across two 12-hour night shifts weekly with a third shared on-call backup for event escalation; budget €50–60K annually for the backup position and weekend coverage.

Staff a single technician per 8-hour night shift with a second part-time technician arriving on-call only if a participant triggers an event alert (motion detector or audio alarm); reduce overnight wages to €25–30K annual savings but accept 10–15 minute response latency to confirmed events.

Operate four dedicated nighttime technician positions on rotating 4-night weeks, pairing two technicians per shift and splitting 8-suite coverage between them (4 suites per person, light annotation burden per person); reallocate contingency budget to cover €40K annual increase; reduce turnover and enable dual real-time annotation.
review: The core tension is between safety-first response capability and sustainable labor economics: the plan explicitly requires adequate margin for event response and annotation, yet 3 full-time technicians covering 8 suites continuously leaves minimal slack for illness, turnover, or algorithm-triggered false alarms. Paired teams solve the problem but double the budget line for this function, crowding out other year-two priorities (publishing, algorithm development, equipment replacement). None of the options quantify the facility's acceptable time-to-response for a participant with high fall or self-injury risk, nor do they specify the criteria for escalating an event alert to emergency services or on-site intervention.

name: Matched Control Recruitment and Monitoring Protocol
decision_axis: This lever controls benchmark validity and recruitment complexity by choosing between no control group, minimal controls (5–10 healthy sleepers, one brief stay each), or full-cohort matched controls (equal n and duration as NREM parasomnia participants).
consequences: No control group eliminates recruitment and facility burden but leaves the event-triage algorithm uncalibrated against normal nocturnal movement, arousal, and sensor artifacts; false-positive rates cannot be assessed, weakening Aim 3 deliverables. Minimal controls (short-stay 'single-night' controls) reduce cost and logistical complexity but provide limited baseline data for cross-participant algorithm generalization; differences in stay duration and acclimation bias the comparison. Full matched-controls (n=25–35, identical suite duration and PSG schedule) provide robust benchmarking and enable assessment of algorithm specificity but adds approximately 25–35 additional participant-nights, raising staffing and annotation burden by 20–30% and requiring separate ethics oversight as a secondary research arm.
options: Recruit 25–30 healthy adult controls (age 25–65, no sleep disorders, no medication affecting sleep) for a single uninterrupted overnight stay with full PSG on admission night and lightweight monitoring resumption on discharge; use their data to establish baseline event-detector false-positive rates and arousal/movement phenotypes.

Forgo a dedicated control group; instead, use polysomnography data from University Hospital Bonn's sleep clinic (age- and sex-matched controls with normal sleep, anonymized) as external reference for algorithm specificity; saves facility recruitment but introduces site effects and lack of sensor-calibration overlap.

Recruit 8–10 healthy controls for week-long stays matching the intake protocol of parasomnia participants (full PSG night 1, mid-stay, discharge); integrate them throughout the 3-year accrual to enable continuous algorithm refinement on control data; accept modest additional stay burden in exchange for robust cross-temporal benchmarking.
review: The core tension is whether the event-triage algorithm must be tested on home-sleep data (controls in the residential suites) or whether external clinic polysomnography can substitute without introducing uncontrolled bias. The plan identifies controls as essential for benchmarking, yet does not specify the algorithm decision threshold (sensitivity vs. specificity trade-off), the acceptable control false-positive rate, or how algorithm retraining on early cohorts might inflate benchmark metrics when tested on later controls. Full matched controls consume facility capacity that could otherwise admit parasomnia participants, directly competing with the Aim 2 enrollment target of 50–70; none of the options quantify the trade-off between phenotyping sample size and algorithm generalization confidence.

name: Publication Strategy and Pre-Publication Data Embargo
decision_axis: This lever controls funding sustainability and first-mover competitive advantage by choosing between rapid single-author papers, phased multi-author publication with embargo delays, or embargo-free open collaboration with external sites.
consequences: Rapid publication (year-one methods paper, year-two phenotyping report) establishes priority and attracts follow-on funding, but risks premature claims about algorithm performance or clinical generalizability, inviting replication challenges. Phased multi-author publication with short embargoes (2–3 months between internal finalization and external team pre-publication, per plan) aligns grant visibility with external partnership but requires careful IP/authorship negotiation and delays dataset utility for external researchers. Embargo-free immediate open sharing (deposit dataset upon publication, pre-print circulation) maximizes reproducibility and citation but surrenders first-mover advantage on secondary analyses and may invite critical technical commentary before internal validation is complete.
options: Publish methods validation paper (Aim 1) in year one in a peer-reviewed sleep medicine journal; publish Aim 2 longitudinal phenotyping report in year two; publish Aim 3 algorithm benchmark paper in year two or three; embargo all raw data until 6 months after final publication to permit follow-up studies.

Adopt a rolling publication model: submit methods papers at month 12, phenotyping at month 18–20, and algorithm benchmark at month 26–28; deposit de-identified dataset to repository upon first publication acceptance, enabling external replication and secondary analyses in parallel with in-house ongoing work.

Coordinate multi-institutional publication consortium: invite 2–3 European sleep research centers to jointly analyze the dataset in a formal collaboration agreement; agree on a single unified publication timeline and authorship structure; distribute dataset and manuscripts for co-submission simultaneously to avoid redundant analyses.
review: The core tension is between establishing scientific priority (rapid publication) and maximizing dataset reuse and external validation (embargo-free open sharing immediately). The plan mandates 2–3 peer-reviewed publications as a success criterion, yet does not specify the timeline for dataset deposition or whether preliminary findings (e.g., preliminary algorithm performance) will be pre-printed to claim priority before peer review completes. If the team publishes methods in year one and phenotyping in year two, but dataset deposition is delayed by embargo, external researchers cannot independently validate claims for many months, slowing reproducibility. Conversely, if data is opened immediately, downstream analyses by external teams may scoop secondary findings (e.g., specific trigger phenotypes, genotype–phenotype associations) that the Bonn team had not yet pursued.

name: Intra-Study Participant Retention and Stay Extension Protocol
decision_axis: This lever controls what triggers a stay extension and how aggressively to retain low-yield participants by choosing between strict protocol adherence, outcome-driven flexibility, or predictive discharge.
consequences: Extending stays for participants who have not yet captured an event increases cumulative event yield and strengthens Aim 2 phenotyping, but consumes facility bed-days that could admit new participants and may create fatigue or protocol fatigue in participants with genuinely infrequent episodes. The decision also affects recruitment messaging — if participants learn they may be asked to stay longer than initially promised, intake willingness may decline.
options: Discharge all participants at the pre-specified end date regardless of event capture, reserving extensions only for participants who have captured at least one event and express written consent to stay longer

Automatically offer two-week stay extensions to all participants with zero captured events in the initial period, provided they meet medical stability criteria and consent, with a weekly re-evaluation process

Deploy a predictive model trained on participant intake characteristics (age, self-reported episode frequency, comorbidities) to flag high-risk non-yielders early and selectively prioritize extensions only for participants predicted to capture events within the extended window
review: The core tension is between maximizing event yield (justifying the facility cost) and maintaining ethical recruitment promises (protecting participant autonomy and trust). All three options assume that extending a participant's stay is medically safe and logistically feasible, but none explicitly addresses the cumulative burnout and protocol fatigue risk — a participant confined for 10 weeks without witnessing their own behavior may experience depression or sleep disruption that itself alters parasomnia frequency, confounding the very phenotype being studied.

name: Real-Time Technician Event Annotation vs. Formal Dual-Rater Workflow
decision_axis: This lever controls how much authority the night technician's real-time judgment carries in identifying parasomnia candidates by choosing between technician-led rapid flagging with post-hoc dual review, full dual-rater consensus before any downstream use, or hybrid signal escalation.
consequences: Rapid technician flagging enables near-immediate escalation deployment (full PSG capture) on subsequent nights when recurrence is likely, maximizing physiological detail for rare events. However, if the technician misses events or over-flags false positives, downstream analyses and algorithm training become noisy. Insisting on dual-rater consensus before triggering escalations guarantees rigor but may delay escalation nights beyond the optimal window if event recurrence is unpredictable or infrequent.
options: Empower the night technician to flag probable parasomnia candidates in real time for escalation night deployment; require dual independent raters to formally adjudicate whether each flagged event meets AASM criteria, with technician flags treated as preliminary hypotheses rather than ground truth

Mandate that all escalation-night deployments require agreement between the night technician and one scheduled rater (same-shift or next-morning review) before commitment, ensuring each escalation is deliberate and documented in real time

Route all potential events through a three-tiered filter: technician flags candidates, a second independent scorer reviews video and physiology independently without access to the technician's notes, and only if both agree does escalation deploy on the next opportunity
review: The core tension is responsiveness vs. rigor — escalation nights capture recurrence at high resolution but are expensive (full PSG montage) and bed-nights are finite. All options assume that escalation nights will materially improve event characterization, but none quantifies how often participants actually have a repeat episode within 24–48 hours of the first flagged event, or whether escalation nights on low-yield participants waste resources. The absence of a baseline 'event recurrence rate' from pilot data could make real-time escalation decisions arbitrary.

name: Sensor Middleware Robustness and In-Situ Failure Mode Management
decision_axis: This lever controls how the unit responds to real-time sensor dropout or data corruption by choosing between redundant hardware with automatic fallback, software-only recovery with manual overnight restart, or tiered degradation with partial-montage backup.
consequences: A participant's night of monitoring is lost or severely compromised if a critical sensor (EEG headband, mattress pressure, time-sync camera) fails and there is no fallback. Redundancy (dual headband sets, backup mattress sensors) increases equipment cost and setup complexity but ensures that a single component failure does not void the night's data. Software-only recovery minimizes upfront hardware cost but may lose data during the failure-to-restart window and places burden on an already-stretched night technician.
options: Equip each sleep suite with primary and hot-standby sensor sets (two EEG headbands, two mattress sensors, two synchronized infrared cameras) that can be swapped within minutes; develop a pre-recorded automated backup startup sequence that the technician can trigger if primary hardware fails, ensuring continuous time-synchronized data streams

Maintain single sensor sets per suite with automated data-integrity checks running in real time; if a failure is detected, alert the technician immediately and provide a documented protocol for safe emergency restart (power-cycling, re-sync) without requiring spare hardware, accepting a small window of data loss during troubleshooting

Deploy a tiered degradation mode: if a non-critical sensor (ambient microphone, environmental sensor) fails, continue with reduced montage; if a critical sensor (EEG, camera) fails, shift to a lightweight backup montage (dry electrode EEG only, no video) for the remainder of the night rather than attempting real-time repair
review: The core tension is cost vs. availability — redundant hardware is expensive and storage-intensive, but a single night of sensor failure can invalidate weeks of enrollment effort. All three options assume that technician response is rapid and reliable, but the unit is staffed for participant safety and event response, not continuous IT troubleshooting; if a sensor fails during a high-intensity event, the technician may not be available to restart. Additionally, none of the options addresses cascading failures or the risk that a backup sensor was silently miscalibrated or drifted since its last-used date.

name: Night Shift Coverage Model and Technician Continuity
decision_axis: This lever controls how the unit maintains continuous 24/7 technician presence by choosing between fixed rotating team, on-call hybrid staffing, or external contracted overnight monitoring.
consequences: A fixed rotating team (3 technicians covering 8 suites via scheduled rotations) ensures deep familiarity with participant safety protocols, sensor systems, and event recognition patterns, reducing costly errors and false negatives. However, technician burnout in overnight work is high; maintaining scheduling equity, managing leave, and absorbing turnover is administratively complex. On-call hybrid models (fewer full-time night staff supplemented by part-time call-in technicians) reduce baseline salary burden but increase response latency and knowledge fragmentation if call-in technicians are less experienced.
options: Hire 3 dedicated overnight research technicians working fixed night shift rotations (e.g., 2-on/3-off or similar pattern) with sleep-disorder clinical training provided in-house, investing in fatigue-management support and clear advancement pathways to reduce turnover and build a specialized team

Employ 1.5 full-time overnight technicians covering core hours (midnight to 6 a.m.) plus on-call backup from a pool of trained part-time evening/early-morning staff, reducing salary overhead and allowing flexibility to scale staffing during low-census periods

Contract overnight coverage through a regional healthcare staffing agency with standing agreements for sleep-monitoring competency; retain one in-house lead technician for protocol oversight and escalations, outsourcing the routine 24/7 coverage to agency personnel trained against the unit's Standard Operating Procedures
review: The core tension is specialization vs. cost — a dedicated night team builds expertise and continuity but is expensive and vulnerable to burnout or unexpected turnover. All three options assume that night technicians are interchangeable after training, but parasomnia event recognition requires pattern familiarity that may take months to develop; a new or agency technician may miss subtle behavioral cues that a veteran technician would flag immediately. Additionally, on-call and agency models create liability risks if an external technician fails to escalate an event or misses a safety concern.

name: External Scientific Advisory Board Scope and Decision Authority
decision_axis: This lever controls the advisory board's role in research direction and operational pivots by choosing between advisory-only review, binding veto over major decisions, or tiered authority based on decision category.
consequences: An advisory-only board (current structure) preserves PI operational autonomy and allows rapid mid-course corrections, but may miss external perspective on methodological risks or reputational issues. A board with binding veto authority over enrollment, escalation criteria, or data-sharing decisions ensures external accountability and peer scrutiny but slows decision-making and may create friction if the PI and board disagree on risk tolerance or scientific priority.
options: Retain the current advisory structure with semi-annual review; expand the board charter to require written board recommendation (not veto) on any protocol amendment affecting participant safety, enrollment criteria, or data retention, with the PI required to document the board's input and rationale for any decision that deviates from the recommendation

Grant the advisory board binding veto authority over changes to participant inclusion/exclusion criteria, stay extension decisions affecting more than 20% of enrolled cohort, and any data-sharing agreement outside the de-identified physiological dataset, while preserving PI authority over day-to-day operational staffing and scheduling

Establish a three-tier decision authority: Tier 1 (operational staffing, participant scheduling) remains with the PI with post-hoc board review; Tier 2 (recruitment pathway, event-triage algorithm changes, publication strategy) requires board consensus recommendation; Tier 3 (participant safety protocol, data governance, major budget reallocations) requires board approval before implementation
review: The core tension is autonomy vs. oversight — tight PI control enables agility but risks blind spots in a novel clinical environment; board authority ensures peer accountability but may paralyze decision-making or create defensiveness. All three options assume that the external board members are sufficiently engaged and informed to make substantive recommendations on early-stage methodology, but most board members will only see 6-month summary reports and may lack real-time visibility into emerging risks or unexpected participant behaviors.

name: Facility Lease Structure and Long-Term Venue Commitment
decision_axis: This lever controls the duration and flexibility of the physical facility lease by choosing between short-term flexible terms, multi-year locked commitment, or partnership with the hospital.
consequences: A multi-year locked lease provides stability for renovation investment and staff hiring, enabling long-term facility development and participant recruitment messaging ('we will be here for your full study'). However, if operational challenges emerge (inadequate acoustic isolation, persistent false alarms, poor participant recruitment), the unit is locked into expensive space and cannot pivot to an alternative location. Flexible short-term leases allow rapid exit if the model fails but provide weak incentive for the landlord to invest in necessary safety modifications or negotiate favorable terms.
options: Negotiate a 3-year renewable lease with the property owner, conditional on completion of the safety and infrastructure renovation, with break clauses if enrollment or event-yield targets fall below protocol thresholds at month 18, protecting both the unit's operational flexibility and the landlord's revenue

Secure a formal partnership with University Hospital Bonn to house the unit in a hospital-owned or hospital-controlled facility (such as a clinic satellite building), consolidating lease negotiations through the university and leveraging existing hospital oversight and emergency-response infrastructure

Establish a short-term (12-month) lease with a month-to-month renewal option after year one, reducing upfront commitment and allowing relocation if early operations reveal unmanageable environmental or participant-recruitment barriers, accepting higher base rent and less landlord willingness to fund renovations
review: The core tension is commitment vs. optionality — a locked long-term lease is expensive if the facility model proves unworkable, but a flexible short-term lease signals uncertainty to participants and staff, undermining recruitment and team morale. All three options assume that the Bonn neighborhood property is suitable for the unit's needs (safety modifications feasible, acoustic isolation achievable, proximity to recruitment channels), but none addresses the risk that renovation costs will exceed budget or uncover structural hazards that force relocation mid-project.

name: Publication Sequencing and Results Embargo Strategy
decision_axis: This lever controls the timing and content of early publications by choosing between methods-first rapid reporting, outcome-first phenotyping focus, or embargo-then-release strategy with pharma partnership option.
consequences: Publishing methods validation and safety outcomes early (year 1–2) establishes the facility's technical credibility and attracts secondary research collaborations, but risks competitors developing parallel infrastructure or competitors using published protocols without data-sharing reciprocity. Delaying all publications until longitudinal phenotyping data mature (year 3) maximizes scientific impact and ensures robustness, but creates publication drought during years 1–2 that may frustrate funders and team morale and miss early-career publication opportunities for postdocs.
options: Publish a dedicated methods paper (year 1.5) covering facility design, sensor integration, safety protocols, and inter-rater reliability validation with the pilot cohort; hold phenotyping results confidential until completion of all 50–70 NREM participant data collection (year 2.5), then release longitudinal characterization and algorithm benchmarking in simultaneous submissions to high-impact journals

Release a brief operational feasibility report in year 1 (demonstrating safe 6-month operations, recruitment success, and false-alarm rates), followed by methods and preliminary phenotyping data in year 2, and comprehensive longitudinal results in year 3, spreading visibility and allowing iterative incorporation of early feedback into ongoing data collection

If a pharmaceutical partnership is secured, adopt a pre-publication comment window (30 days) for all planned publications, allowing the industry partner early sight of results while maintaining full academic publication rights; if no partnership materializes, publish methods in year 1.5 and phenotyping in year 3 without embargo
review: The core tension is visibility vs. protection — early publication stakes early claims and builds credibility but exposes novel methodology to external scrutiny and reduces competitive advantage; delayed publication maximizes rigor but may feel unfamiliar or discouraging to a young team and funders expecting interim milestones. None of the three options explicitly addresses the risk that early-stage methodology papers, while well-intentioned, may be cited as if the facility is a 'mature validated platform' rather than a developmental infrastructure project, overselling the maturity of the work to downstream researchers or clinicians.

name: Matched Control Recruitment and Baseline Monitoring Depth
decision_axis: This lever controls the scope and sampling intensity of matched control participants by choosing between full-study matched pairs, separate control cohort with lighter monitoring, or algorithm-only controls from public datasets.
consequences: Recruiting and monitoring a full matched control group (1:1 or similar ratio) alongside the NREM parasomnia cohort provides high-quality baseline comparators for algorithm development and behavioral phenotyping, but doubles participant volume and associated costs (compensation, technician time, data storage). Using lighter monitoring for controls (fewer PSG nights, shorter stays) reduces cost but may introduce systematic measurement error if controls are monitored under different protocols, confounding sensitivity/specificity estimates for the event-triage tool.
options: Recruit matched controls (n=25–35, matched on age, sex, habitual sleep schedule, and BMI range) and monitor them under identical monitoring protocols as the NREM cohort (tiered sensor, enhanced nights, escalation nights if atypical movements are detected), enabling direct comparison of normal nocturnal movement patterns and sensor false-positive rates

Recruit a smaller control group (n=10–15) and monitor them via low-burden continuous sensing (EEG headband, mattress sensor, camera only) without enhanced PSG nights or escalation nights, reducing control costs while capturing baseline sensor variability and false-positive rates for algorithm training

Retain a local cohort of controls monitored in the facility for one night only under full PSG montage to establish baseline movement and arousal patterns, supplemented by openly available polysomnography datasets from public repositories to increase statistical power for algorithm benchmarking without internal control overhead
review: The core tension is sensitivity to signal vs. cost-efficiency — full matched controls provide unambiguous comparison but consume facility capacity; lighter-burden controls reduce cost but may not adequately characterize the baseline false-positive rate of the sensor suite. All three options assume that matched controls will tolerate multiple nights of residential monitoring and accept the inconvenience without compensation differential, and that 'matching' on demographics is sufficient to account for behavioral differences between individuals with known parasomnias and healthy sleepers — but sleep behavior itself is highly individual, and a young healthy control may have qualitatively different nocturnal movement or arousal patterns than a similarly aged person with chronic sleep disorders, regardless of demographic matching.

name: Participant Stay Duration and Event-Capture Yield Commitment
decision_axis: This lever controls how strictly the protocol enforces minimum event capture by choosing between fixed-duration stays, adaptive extensions based on early yield signals, and probability-weighted stay recommendations informed by weekly predictive models.
consequences: Fixed stays simplify staffing and budget predictability but accept zero-event admissions and reduce publication-ready data. Adaptive extensions with early-yield prediction can boost event capture rates by allowing rapid extension decisions (e.g., by day 10 rather than day 14), but require accurate forecasting models and introduce unequal stay lengths that complicate cross-participant analyses. Probability-weighted recommendations (presenting participants with evidence-based extension odds) respect autonomy and reduce administrative burden on technicians, though they risk fewer extensions and lower per-participant yield if participants decline based on personal constraints.
options: Enforce a strict minimum stay of 21 days followed by an optional two-week extension only for participants with zero events, regardless of early event-capture trends.

Implement a rolling predictive model that flags participants by day 7 with low event probability (based on baseline arousal rates and reported frequency history) and recommends (but does not mandate) expedited two-week or four-week extensions before day 10.

Offer all participants a transparent one-page probability sheet by day 10 showing their personal event-capture trajectory versus cohort norms, with extension availability contingent on their informed choice rather than protocol-driven triggers.
review: The core tension is between maximizing productive data per admission slot (which favors aggressive extensions) and protecting participant wellbeing and operational stability (which favors predictable durations). None of the options address the interaction between extension fatigue and behavioral suppression — a participant extended into week five may show fewer genuine parasomnias due to homesickness or facility habituation, paradoxically reducing the very yield the extension sought to capture.

name: Inter-Rater Scoring Workflow Parallelization and Timing
decision_axis: This lever controls when independent scorers receive videos and what information they see by choosing between sequential scoring of complete stays, parallel split-file scoring with no crosstalk, or staged scoring where a threshold-check pass gates advance to full adjudication.
consequences: Sequential scoring (scorer A then B) preserves independence but delays publication and wastes scorer B's time if errors are caught late. Parallel split-file scoring (scorer A rates nights 1–4, scorer B rates nights 5–8) improves timeline but introduces site-of-night bias and complicates inter-rater reliability reporting across the same participant. Staged scoring (technician flags candidate events, both scorers rapidly assess flagged windows, then formal full-night scoring if agreement emerges) accelerates time-to-adjudication and reduces wasted scoring effort on low-signal nights, but requires explicit criteria for the threshold check and risks gatekeeping true events if the threshold is too strict.
options: Conduct full sequential dual scoring with scorer independence enforced: scorer A completes all nights for a participant, then scorer B independently scores the same nights without access to A's marks, followed by consensus adjudication of disagreements.

Split each participant's stay into two time-blocked subsets (early and late stay) with scorer A assigned to early nights and scorer B to late nights, both scoring in parallel with baseline inter-rater reliability calculated from a held-out validation cohort scored by both raters.

Institute a two-stage workflow where technician event flags undergo rapid 10-minute consensus evaluation by both scorers on flagged windows only; if both agree on presence/absence, the judgment is adjudicated; if disagreement, full night-by-night dual scoring is performed to resolve the discrepancy.
review: The central tension is balancing timeline pressure (publications require timely adjudication) against methodological rigor (inter-rater reliability is a core outcome). The staged workflow reduces wasted effort but creates a hidden dependency: if the threshold for triggering full scoring is calibrated on early participants, systematic drift in technician flagging sensitivity later in the study could bias which admissions receive rigorous double-review.

name: Night Technician Cross-Coverage Redundancy and Skill Depth
decision_axis: This lever controls how many technicians are trained to full operational independence and how many nights per week each works by choosing between a single-lead-technician model with specialized backups, a rotational three-person peer model with equal capability, or a hybrid where one senior technician mentors two junior technicians on staggered shift exposure.
consequences: Single-lead with backups concentrates expertise but creates critical-person dependency; if the lead is unavailable, backup technicians may miss subtle event signs or trigger false alarms at higher rates, degrading data quality and staff confidence. Rotational peer model spreads risk and improves morale through equal responsibility, but requires higher total training investment and may dilute event detection consistency if individual technicians develop idiosyncratic annotation styles. Hybrid senior-mentorship model preserves expertise depth while building redundancy through graduated responsibility; the tradeoff is slower onboarding and potential friction between senior authority and junior autonomy, especially during high-stress multi-event nights.
options: Designate one senior full-time night technician as the operational lead, present five nights per week with two trained backup technicians covering one night each; backups shadow the lead every other week and handle only routine monitoring, alarm response reserved for the lead.

Rotate three technicians equally on a three-week cycle: weeks 1 and 2 each technician works four nights covering rotating suites, week 3 each works two nights with another technician co-present; all three attend a bi-weekly joint competency review and annotation calibration meeting.

Hire a senior sleep technician (lead) to work three nights per week plus administrative mentorship duties, with two junior technicians each working three nights per week in a supervised rotation where the senior technician is on-call via phone and visits the facility weekly to co-score annotations and provide real-time feedback.
review: The fundamental tension is between cost (the rotational peer model requires three full salaries) and safety (single-lead model creates dangerous bottlenecks). None of the options explicitly address what happens on a night when the lead technician is ill and both backups are simultaneously unavailable — the facility would be forced to close, and no option pre-positions contingency staffing (e.g., standby agreements with other local sleep labs or cross-training of non-sleep staff as emergency monitors).

name: Video Privacy Tiering and Selective Dataset Sequestration
decision_axis: This lever controls how strictly bedroom video is segregated from scientific data streams by choosing between complete video isolation with manual-only review, encrypted on-site-only access with audit trails, or selective frame-by-face-obscured video release for methodology transparency.
consequences: Complete isolation (video never leaves the secured facility workstation) maximizes privacy and simplifies ethics compliance but prevents independent verification of technician or scorer judgment calls, limiting external methodological scrutiny. On-site encrypted access with audit trails allows external auditors or co-investigators to verify annotation decisions without storing video off-site, but requires dedicated facility space and visitor coordination, creating operational friction. Selective obscured-face release (redacting faces while preserving posture and limb movement) enables transparency and could support algorithm development on real behavioral data, but introduces new regulatory risk (redaction failure, re-identification via gait or room features) and may itself trigger participant privacy concerns.
options: Maintain all bedroom video exclusively on a facility-locked workstation with restricted physical access; only the night technician, PI, and designated scorers may review video in real time or during annotation, with no remote access and a written audit log of every access date and accessor.

Deploy encrypted video storage on the local NAS with role-based access controls (technician and scorer roles with decryption keys held in separate escrow); allow external scientific auditors to review video on-site via a supervised facility terminal with session recording and subsequent deletion, generating a digital manifest of what was reviewed without copying files.

Implement automated face obscuration on all video using a lightweight neural network model before storage, then deposit the redacted video alongside physiological data in the de-identified repository; conduct a separate privacy validation audit with external consultants to quantify re-identification risk before public release.
review: The core tension is between scientific transparency (external audits strengthen credibility) and privacy protection (video of sleep abnormalities in a residential setting is inherently sensitive). The on-site audit model assumes external parties will travel to Bonn repeatedly for verification, which is logistically expensive and may never happen; selective obscuration sounds protective but shifts privacy risk to the accuracy of the redaction algorithm itself—if the model fails on 2% of frames, a skilled adversary might reconstruct faces from temporal sequences.

name: Pharmaceutical Industry Partnership Scope and Publication Gate
decision_axis: This lever controls the timing and scope of pharma input to results by choosing between a strict arms-length partnership with only a comment window, embedded-partner co-authorship on selected outputs, or no pharmaceutical partnership and full academic autonomy.
consequences: Comment-window-only partnerships (30-day pre-publication review) maintain publication independence but may miss opportunities for industry insight into mechanistic findings or real-world application; the pharma partner gains limited leverage, reducing incentive to fund beyond the baseline €800K. Co-authorship partnerships accelerate manuscript development and signal academic-industry collaboration, attractive to funders, but introduce implicit pressure to frame results favorably toward the partner's drug class or mechanism, potentially delaying publication of null or negative findings. No pharmaceutical partnership preserves full autonomy and simplifies governance but foregoes potential additional revenue and may limit the facility's competitiveness for phase-two funding if a partner's phase-three trial requires your baseline phenotyping data.
options: Accept pharmaceutical partnership as a funded partner with the right to co-author one results manuscript per year if they contribute substantive scientific input to interpretation; retain PI publication authority over other outputs and maintain a 30-day pre-publication comment window for all outputs before journal submission.

Pursue funding exclusively through DFG and university sources, explicitly declining pharmaceutical partnerships to eliminate any perception of bias; frame this independence as a competitive advantage in grant submissions and recruitment messaging to participants who may be hesitant about industry involvement.

Establish a limited consulting relationship where a pharma partner provides €200K–300K in year-two conditional funding in exchange for a structured six-month embargo on results publication post-analysis, allowing them first-look rights for business development; publications proceed thereafter unconditionally.
review: The central tension is between funding security (pharma money accelerates equipment purchases and technician hiring) and reputational independence (parasomnia treatment is competitive, and early industry association risks labeling the research as biased toward specific drug classes). None of the options address what happens if preliminary findings contradict the partner's lead compound's mechanism or show unexpected off-target effects — reputational and contractual conflict is possible, and no option pre-positions a dispute resolution or early termination clause.

name: Semi-Automated Event Pre-Screening Investment and Validation Timeline
decision_axis: This lever controls when and how intensively you develop the triage algorithm by choosing between deferring algorithm work to year three (after sufficient labeled data), beginning algorithm prototyping in year two on data subset, or commissioning an external AI vendor to accelerate the process with a ready-made model.
consequences: Deferring to year three reduces year-one and year-two complexity and allows algorithm development to be informed by the full cohort's event morphologies, increasing final quality; the downside is that technicians continue manual review at full intensity through year two, consuming scorer time and delaying phase-two funding applications which may mention algorithm benchmarking. Year-two prototyping on a subset (e.g., first 20 participants) begins in-house development early, supporting phase-two proposals and reducing manual review burden by mid-year two, but the algorithm trained on 20 participants may not generalize well to new admissions, creating validation headaches. Vendor outsourcing accelerates time-to-prototype (6–8 weeks versus 6 months in-house) and introduces external expertise, but transfers code ownership and interpretability to a third party; black-box vendor models hinder scientific understanding of failure modes and create dependency on vendor maintenance.
options: Allocate algorithm development as a year-three activity to be led by the computational neuroscience postdoc once at least 50 adjudicated events across 30–40 participants are available; design the year-three scope to include sensitivity/specificity benchmarking against dual-scorer agreement and a pre-registered publication plan.

Begin algorithm prototyping in month 15 (early year two) using a supervised learning approach on the first 20 participant cohort's scored events; adopt an in-house Python-based pipeline with the data engineer and postdoc as leads, committing to publish the model's performance metrics against holdout test set even if accuracy is modest (e.g., 65–75% sensitivity).

Engage an external machine learning consulting firm (e.g., a university ML group or boutique biomedical AI consultancy) in month 12 to conduct a 12-week prototype engagement using your annotated data from the pilot cohort; negotiate an academic license for the resulting model code and require publication co-authorship as part of the engagement terms.
review: The core tension is between methodological rigor (building an algorithm from your own data, understanding its failure modes) and time-to-value (external expertise or deferral accelerates other workstreams). Year-two prototyping on a small subset carries an underappreciated risk: if the algorithm performs well on those 20 participants but poorly on admissions 21–50, you may spend months retraining and lose the credibility gain that early algorithm publication would have brought to phase-two proposals.

name: Facility Expansion Staging and Multi-Site Replication Pathway
decision_axis: This lever controls whether future growth is vertical (adding suites 9–12 in Bonn) or horizontal (partnering with other German sleep centers) by choosing between facility-led organic expansion, a formal multi-site consortium model, or a franchise-training approach where partner sites adopt your protocols independently.
consequences: Organic expansion (adding suites 9–12 in Bonn) concentrates operations knowledge and maintains PI direct oversight, simplifying quality control; however, facility real estate in Bonn may become saturated, and participant recruitment from a single region plateaus. Multi-site consortium (partnering with sleep centers in Berlin, Munich, Hamburg) multiplies recruitment reach and event-capture diversity across geographic regions and sleep clinic populations, supporting larger phase-two proposals; the challenge is harmonizing technician training, equipment specifications, and annotation standards across sites with different institutional cultures. Franchise-training (publishing your standard operating procedures and certifying external sites as implementation partners without formal consortium governance) maximizes reach but dilutes your direct quality assurance; sites may diverge from protocols, and you lose claims of uniform data provenance.
options: Commit to expanding the Bonn facility to 10–12 active suites by year three if pilot success metrics are met; conduct a site-feasibility study in month 18 to assess whether additional property renovation is cost-effective relative to participant recruitment plateau and technician supervision scalability.

Initiate partnership discussions by month 12 with two to three established sleep centers (e.g., University Hospital Berlin, Ludwig Maximilian University Munich) to form a formal parasomnia research consortium with a shared protocol, equipment procurement, and technician cross-training; structure governance as a steering committee with quarterly meetings and harmonized data-sharing agreements.

Develop a detailed standard operating procedure manual and technician certification curriculum by end of year two, then pilot a train-the-trainer model with one partner site (e.g., a collaborating German sleep center) in year three; allow the partner site to operate independently with periodic (quarterly) remote audit of annotation samples and event-capture rates.
review: The fundamental tension is between control (organic expansion lets you maintain tight quality standards) and scale (consortium or franchise models multiply events and publications per unit investment). None of the options explicitly address the financial incentive structure for partner sites — if partner sites are expected to adopt your protocols without direct funding, they must absorb costs from their own budgets, and motivation to maintain fidelity erodes over time, especially if their local patient population's event-capture yield is lower than Bonn's.

name: Phase-Two Funding Proposal Timing and Preliminary Results Strategy
decision_axis: This lever controls when you submit the next major grant by choosing between submitting a phase-two proposal by month 18 (before full interim analyses are public), publishing key interim results by month 20 and then proposing, or waiting for full three-year results maturity before proposing a separate intervention or mechanistic follow-up study.
consequences: Early submission (month 18) allows phase-two funding to begin by year three, accelerating expansion and securing continued staff employment; the risk is that reviewers may view preliminary data as insufficient proof-of-concept, or worse, funding is rejected and reapplication is delayed by 6–12 months. Publishing interim results before month-18 proposals strengthens subsequent grant applications (reviewers see published evidence of operational success and event-capture adequacy) but loses the advantage of surprise novelty; competitors may launch similar studies based on your published methods. Waiting for full maturity (36+ months) yields the strongest final dataset but risks a funding gap: year-three continuation funding is conditional on progress, but if phase-two proposals are submitted at month 28, review and decision timelines push funding start to month 36+, potentially creating a 3–6 month operational hiatus.
options: Submit a phase-two infrastructure and expansion proposal by month 18 (concurrent with month-12 published interim methods paper) requesting support for suites 9–12 and a 50-participant expanded cohort; frame the proposal as contingent on preliminary event-capture and operational-safety data from the pilot, with contingency plan for scaled-back expansion if interim results are weak.

Publish two interim papers by month 22 (one on methods validation and one on longitudinal parasomnia characterization from first 30 participants), then submit a phase-two proposal by month 24 requesting augmented computational resources and external validation partners; position the phase-two work as mechanistic depth rather than replication.

Complete full three-year data collection and analysis by month 36, then submit a separate phase-two proposal (month 37+) focused on targeted intervention (e.g., cognitive-behavioral intervention for parasomnia triggers, safety-device prototyping) informed by the completed phenotyping; accept the likelihood of a 6–12 month funding gap and plan contingency staffing reductions accordingly.
review: The core tension is between time-to-funding (early proposals secure continued resources) and evidence strength (publications and completed analyses make stronger proposals). The early-submission strategy assumes reviewers will tolerate preliminary data; if they demand published results, you've submitted prematurely. The published-results strategy assumes your interim papers will not be scooped or misinterpreted by competitors who launch their own parasomnia studies — once your methods are public, the field becomes more competitive, potentially eroding the novelty advantage of your expanded phase-two proposal.
